findings.description: Section headings in PDF are underlined and centered (e.g., 'Introduction', 'Background', 'Results', 'Summary'), while in HTML they are left-aligned, larger, and not underlined.
findings.evidence: PDF shows centered, underlined headings. HTML shows left-aligned headings with larger font and no underline.

findings.description: Title block in PDF is centered with distinct spacing between title, date, and program name. HTML has a horizontal rule under the title and left-aligned date/program name.
findings.evidence: PDF: centered title block. HTML: left-aligned with a horizontal divider line.

findings.description: Map 1 image in PDF shows the map with a property table inset on the right side plus treatment period labels. In HTML, the same image appears but is duplicated across pages (shown on both page 5 in PDF context and the HTML page showing Map 1). The duplicate is acceptable as page break handling, but the image appears correctly.
findings.evidence: PDF page 5 has Map 1 with property table inset. HTML renders the same image with property table.

findings.description: Table 1 in PDF is a compact table with three columns (Name, Y, X). HTML renders it as a wider table with the same structure but more horizontal spacing.
findings.evidence: Both versions contain the same data and column structure; only visual width differs.

findings.description: Map 2 in PDF is a single large image on one page. In HTML it is split across page boundaries but the full image is present.
findings.evidence: PDF: single page map. HTML: map starts on one page section and continues, but all content is present.

findings.description: Diagram 1 in PDF shows three Biobase screenshots arranged in a 2+1 layout (two on top, one centered below) with labels. HTML renders them sequentially in a vertical stack.
findings.evidence: PDF: 2-up + 1 centered layout for Diagram 1. HTML: vertical sequential layout with images and labels interspersed.

findings.description: Table 2 (Secchi Disk) is rendered correctly in both versions with matching data (3 sites, latitude, longitude, Secchi reading).
findings.evidence: Both contain identical data: Site 1 (2.5 ft), Site 2 (3 ft), Site 3 (3 ft).

findings.description: The HTML has a nested <main> element inside another <main> element, which is semantically incorrect HTML structure, though it doesn't affect content fidelity.
findings.evidence: The HTML contains <main> nested inside another <main> element.

findings.description: The document has a duplicate nested <main> tag which could be considered structural duplication, but no actual content is duplicated.
findings.evidence: Two <main> opening tags appear in the HTML structure.
